text:
In-Context Learning (ICL) — What is it? This paper is a survey on In-Context Learning. In-Context Learning refers to a class of recent developments in large language models (LLMs) that can perform novel tasks and behave in new ways upon receiving only a few contextual demonstration examples at inference time, all without the need for model retraining or updating parameters. With ever-increasing size of pre-trained LLMs, ICL has become a de facto way to expand and evaluate the functionalities of LLMs.

In this survey, we first formally define and introduce ICL (Imitation with few Clips) and position it in the context of few-shot learning and prompt tuning. We then structurally organize the field of ICL into three major sub-fields via three primary axes: (i) the Source of Demonstrations, (ii) the learning paradigms, and (iii) the underlying ML frameworks.
1. - **Model Training**: Potential improvements to ICL using pretraining objectives or "warmup" objectives (instruction tuning).
2. Prompt Strategy Techniques for demonstration organization, including techniques for unsupervised and supervised filtering of demonstrations, formatting prompts, and choosing the optimal demonstration order for optimal performance.
3. Annotated bibliography on Analysis and Mechanisms Research questions for this part include: analysis of the impact of various factors on the performance of ICL, such as data distribution and model architecture, as well as developing new theories that explain ICL from a Bayesian perspective or as an instance of implicit gradient descent.

This work surveys, implements and applies a broad range of existing techniques for leveraging context in LLMs in the present moment, across multiple applications from data engineering to multi-modal vision and speech processing tasks. Ultimately, it sketches out the underlying challenges related to, for example, efficiency, scalability and the nature of long distance contextual reasoning that may need to be addressed in order to gain a deeper understanding of how current LLMs actually make use of context and in turn, how these models can be further refined to improve overall performance.
